author: @nielskastor8632
text:
I usually enjoy your videos but this one hits really far from the mark!
A more apt parallel to draw to the "big stink"  is the current existential crisis of humans being cooked alive by global warming as a result of primitive oil burning behavior (have you seen Carl Sagan testifying before the U. S. Congress 50 years ago about this?) , and other lovely effects of fossil fuel consumption such as vastly increasing cancer rates from seriously nasty stuff like p-fast chemicals,  microplastics in human breast milk and every organism living in the ocean (just for starers).
All completely unnecessary.
Do you actually know anyone who has their car scrapped after 3.5 years? 
I have yet to meet this mythical beast.
As to propagating big oil propaganda, where do you come up with your data on electric vehicle maintenance costs? Certainly not from anyone who actually owns one. The truth is maintenance cost is vastly lower than i.c.e., as there are no tune ups required, almost no break system wear because of regenerative braking, almost no moving parts, the list goes on and on.
A different perspective is that we are in the beginning of the clean energy era, remember when the gasoline powered automobile was a new thing? There were a few developments over time in the petroleum era, and taking into account the exponential progress of technology, it will not take long to address any remaining issues with electrifying everything, notwithstanding the constant onslaught of disinformation. 
The time when "driving less" could make an impact has long past as the tipping point has already peaked.
If you are not interested in maintaining even a slim hope for a desirable future for the human species, why are you into playing with clean energy projects?

replies.author: @GarlandFarms
replies.text:
Almost two-thirds (63.3%) of global electricity comes from fossil fuels. 
To manufacture a two-megawatt windmill, you would need 260 tonnes of steel, 300 tonnes of iron ore, and 170 tonnes of coking coal. The production of these materials requires the use of hydrocarbons, which are fossil fuels. The amount of fossil fuels required to manufacture wind turbines is equivalent to more than 600 million metric tons of coal.
Wake up or grow up.

author: @scottrauenhorst1342
text: Good video slight disagreement with the average age of vehicles due to the exponential increase in production recently. 3.8 years will prove to be much shorter than true life of vehicle. What needs to be analyzed is percent of EV's that are no longer on the road compared to number of EVs produced over the last 5 or so years (beginning of high volume production) and compare that to the same statistics of all other vehicles. This percent of cars on the road statistic will be less influenced by changes in production rate; it could be argued that you also need to correct for vehicles that are not on the road due to crashes since the main EVs, Teslas, have a much low crash rate per mile. Unfortunately, we need to see EV production stabilize before we can determine proper life of the vehicle.

author: @sagelikea6130
text:
Comparing the issue of EVs to the Great Stink is a big stretch. It's not a matter of lack of infrastructure for EVs or other so-called 'green' tech none of which is green or sustainable btw. 

EVs are an evolutionary dead end and so is large scale wind and solar. I live in Alberta which is the sunniest and windiest place in Canada and wind and solar come no where close to being efficient sources of energy. They produce a pittance of our energy requirements when they do manage to produce, a single gas well with a very small footprint can produce far more energy than 100s of acres of solar and wind and does reliably and cleanly. Large scale wind farms are ugly monstrosities and a blight on the landscape and so is solar.

And just look at the Luton fire which burned 1600 cars because a hybrid Rover caught fire. It was the Battery and when batteries burn they produce far more toxic fumes than any ICE car would. Or how about the truck fires in Australia. In Edmonton, their EV public transit experiment was an epic failure and it was predictable because government was involved. Insurance costs are higher because repairs are more expensive and when they catch fire, the fires are catastrophic. EVs are at least 20% heavier so accidents will also be worse and infrastructure will solve none of these issues. 

In the past few months, I've seen two completely burned out Amazon delivery vans. Lithium battery products no doubt. The list goes on and on. 

I'm all for solar and wind on a small residential or industrial scale and hope to install solar and wind on my home with the goal of going off-grid and not because I think Earth is facing some climate apocalypse. It is not and anyone with a shred of objectivity can see the global warming is a hoax. I want to go off-grid because the world is run by idiots intent on destroying everything that made the west great including cheap energy. I also like the idea of tinkering with this tech but it really only makes sense on a small scale and most importantly.

Hydrogen makes much more sense than EVs and honestly, there isn't nearly enough copper in the world to make EVs a practical reality.

I enjoy watching your videos and learn a lot but please, lets be honest about so-called 'green tech' and global warming which is yet another manufactured issue from Regressives.

author: @finndriver1063
text:
I'm pretty convinced, to the degree that I don't consider it conspiratorial, that the electric car is supposed to save the car companies, not the planet or the people on it. When you can't ignore the problems with cars any longer, why not buy one with sheep's clothing pre-installed? The solution is better public transport, and greater priority for public transport.

The interesting thing to me is that there are only a few rich-and-powerful nations that are truly pro-car, and they're (descending order) the US, Canada, UK. We should be on the bleeding edge of transport, just to catch up! 
Instead in the UK, although we have one of the biggest metro systems of any city, an okay-but-not-good train network, and used to be on the forefront of train travel in general, we redesigned our systems to favour the motorist, and now it's getting increasingly difficult to reverse the effects. 
Italy killed their short-haul flights with high-speed rail, which we can't build because it's an 'environmental disaster' and thus use planes instead. Paris is cleaning the Seine and going from one of the worst cities to cycle in, to one of the best, in just a couple of decades; meanwhile the idea that 20mph is too slow (even if it will save the kids) is just too much to bear for our poor prime minister and needs to be ratified.

Things will get better, they always do, but I'm just not sure we can afford to leave it to the last minute. We might be 'fine' (outside of Hemsby), but people in countries with less socioeconomic power will see mass-tragedy. Plus, not to be cynical, but the longer we leave it, the more expensive it will get to manage and fix.

author: @markottaway6899
text:
Another good video, but the EV part is badly researched and generalised.  Firstly, the growth in recycling EV batteries is limited by the fact they are re-used.  
When the capacity reaches a threshold the battery needs to be replaced, but this is only to mantain range.  These batteries are still 85 to 90% of their rated capacity and are used in the growing energy storage industry.
When a car is scrapped the vehicle goes to a breakers yard and the battery is removed and either sold in modules or complete, its quite a lucrative market.  People either split the cells up and make their own storage systems with a new battery management system or/and use a circuit that translates the communication from the cars BMS so it can be used by normal residential hybrid inverters.  Many examples can be seen with a quick search of youtube.
Maybe you should consider building one, as you have built many things that could charge one.
I have a 2019 Renault Zoe, which I have no plans to replace, lovely car.  The Zoe was one of the first EVs and started production in 2012, Renault has stated that over 90% of Zoes are still on the road.

author: @passiveftp
text: ah yes we should make things last longer but the manufacturers build in something called planned obsolescence, which means the products break after so many years because of the way things are designed and built, meaning you have to buy a new one. For example my tumble dryer drum shaft was restng on a hole in the thin metal at the back of the dryer, so after so many spins the hole wore away and the drum stopped spinning, including the belt snapping - The manufacturer knows that after so many spins it will break and the average number of spins per year so after 3 years it will totally break. Other examples of planned obsolescence are Apple updating their iPhone software to slow down older phones so people had to buy new ones! millions were probably thrown away. What a waste. It's disgusting and all for profit at the cost of the environment.

text: Stopping at 7:04.  The 5% claim is for ALL Li-Ion batteries, not LI-Ion batteries used in EVs. Rob, I don't disagree with your general premise but would disagree with the particular statistics around EV's.   I can only use the 2018 Chevy Volt (plugin hybrid) we purchased in late 2017 as an example.  We've driven it about 140,000 miles, the major on electric and we have no intention to trade it anytime soon.  The battery shows no evidence of degraded performance and there are companies available who will come to your location and do the repairs there. From what I understand most of the batteries are now designed to replace cells or units which greatly reduces costs.
   As far as recycling batteries go, I understand spent lithium batteries are in high demand.

author: @williammeek4078
text:
I am not as pessimistic on the supporting infrastructure for EVs. Repair infrastructure is coming online as we speak and battery recycling is happening. I wonder where you get your 5% number though. That sounds more like the rate of recycling for consumer electronics batteries than traction batteries.
Also, I think you erred on the 3.8 years thing. That is average age. Not average life. 
With the exponential growth curve EVs have seen, the average age has fallen even as average life has gotten longer. 
For example, around 70% of all EVs in the US are Teslas. And nearly all of them are still on the road.

author: @IFRYRCE
text:
As someone who works for an OEM that is attempting (badly) to transition to EVs, I think I can make a few salient points.
The increase in insurance premiums for EVs over ICE is likely here to stay. If there's even a slight risk the battery pack has been damaged, it needs to be replaced. Shops and insurance companies will likely never tolerate even the risk of battery damage due to the immense fire risk batteries pose (at least with current chemistry). There's no single part on an ICE vehicle that is remotely equivalent in cost to an EV battery - in most ICE vehicles you could replace the engine and transmission both and have money left over vs an EV battery replacement. Additionally EVs have a lot of flashy external features at the moment and very expensive external lighting (I'm sure you've all seen the 'light bars' many seem to have in front). That stuff adds immensely to repair cost versus a regular ole headlamp. Plus EVs are heavier by 25-30% versus an equivalent ICE vehicle, so they're just more of a risk from an insurance perspective anyways - they will do more damage to themselves, and whatever they hit. Finally, on collision, many batteries have a fuse that breaks if a bad accident is detected, and there's no way to replace that in most current models. So, in short, merely by getting into an accident, chances are you'll need a battery replacement unless it was a fender bender.
Also, it's insanely hard to keep something as big as a battery pack completely sealed for 10+ years. Think about how long you go in an ICE car before it develops an oil or coolant leak. Now apply that same concept to a battery, only water leaks in instead of oil leaking out. Then fire.
Frankly, I think governments (and companies, some of which are following the government and some of which are leading it...) are betting on the wrong horse in this race. Synthetic fuels from carbon capture and biofuels (preferably made from waste) both seem like a far better option to me than EVs. A synthetic fuel is still a battery, if you stretch the concept a bit. You capture carbon, and use energy input and processing to turn it back into a fuel. Current bleeding edge battery tech is about 25% as energy dense per lb as gas or diesel is, so unless there's a doubling or tripling of battery energy density EVs shouldn't even be looked at as viable.
Hybrids are a whole other story, and it's wild governments are basically ignoring them in favor of EVs. It's the best of both worlds, and the 'two drivetrains' argument really doesn't hold water, because it winds up cheaper than an EV and more efficient than ICE anyways, while being closer to an ICE car in weight than it is to an EV (because the battery is small). 
Finally, from an emissions perspective, (new) ICE vehicles are probably the cleanest way to burn hydrocarbons on the planet at this point. The massive emissions associated with them is because one person per vehicle is inefficient, not because the engines themselves aren't. Since you're not solving the single person transport issue with EVs (and they're heavier), it's not as green as it seems, especially if you compare it to a hybrid that only uses an ICE engine to recharge batteries. That ICE engine is going to pollute WAY less per KW of energy put into that battery than any equivalent fossil fuel power plant. That can even be net zero (or net negative) using captured carbon/bio fuel.

author: @miken7629
text: Don't care if the power-train of a car is electric but Battery EV's are the wrong approach, too limiting, too expensive, requires too much new infrastructure.  They are testing an APU (Auxiliary Power Unit) where is a small jet engine is running a generator, as long as the generator is as big (or bigger) than the drive motor, the APU provides all the power for the vehicle.  If the generator is smaller than drive motor, then limited batteries or super capacitors are needed.  The test car with APU has a 1200 mile range before refueling.  APU's are not new, they exist on airplanes, even the Space Shuttle had APU's.

author: @Johny40Se7en
text:
What a great history lesson. You kept it proper interesting, nice one fella. 
One of the awful things back in those days where sanitation was non existent was the black plague. Rats supposedly lived amongst us for a long time without much issue, but then that plague and other diseases came about and rats spread it, then they got the blame. Poor buggers, even today they have a bad rap...

This video highlights so many other issues which continue to be prevalent, only they're hidden behind facades and indoctrination i.e. factory farming and slaughterhouses. They pollute the rivers to hell with run off of entrails and faeces of other animals, which then flows to the oceans and causes toxic dead zones devoid of life. Instead of showing the public what they're funding and allowing each consumer to make an informed choice, they lie to them to maximize profit.

And don't get me started on single use / throwaway plastic crud still being produced and dumped into oceans or incinerated causing cancerous airborne pollutants. It's one giant vile sh*t show and they get away with it all the time because they mislead people / consumers 🤢🤮

The wealthy can't get away from that either, because they need this Planet to exist too, and last I heard, more than two thirds of this Planet is water, potentially contaminated water by now.

And getting back to the state of electric cars, it's truly bonkers and I love the comparison to the flushing toilet, just moving an issue around. Plug in EV's are a stepping stone, and many people - especially ignorant fanboys / girls - they just don't see it.
And governments support that technology because they can make more money off it quickly, never caring that the materials likely come from truly unethical sources where child labour is common.

Instead, it would be worth focussing resources on the longer term with hydrogen fuel cell infrastructure. Using local renewable energies as much as possible to both create and store the hydrogen on site. If they don't get their arses in gear, it's gonna be another thing which will bite those muppets in their lazy selfish backsides, and worst off, they'll bring the public down with them 😐🥴

They're doing it in other Countries, but true to form, Britain is lagging behind a long way. I mean, come on, Britain is still using diesel trains, vans, trucks and passenger cars FFS, belching out ghastly soot particulates giving kids cancer. Yet the government will try and brush it under the rug like they do with many serious issues. It's pathetic  how they still ramble on about C02 when it's Methane that should be highest problem area to deal with. Sometimes it feels like there's almost no bloody shape or hope for them.

author: @michaelschauperl172
text: From this point of view you speak the truth. But sadly the needs of life often create the need to travel distances. I still own  my old car but in addition I use s electric motorcycle whenever possible. Within one month i drove about one thousand kilometers and saved myself 100€ in fuel. After 20 thousand kilometers I will have the investment back in. The good thing with electric motorcycles as you can charge them on the mains. And I can take the battery out and take it with me. If the prognosis of some of the manufacturers are true that sodium iron iodine  batteries will be available soon and the rise of flexible  Photovoltaik electric Motorcykles could become a kind of enviorment friendly reliable "economical-perpetumobile" (not a physics one)"

replies.author: @erroneouscode
replies.text:
"The people who are against this just need to spend some time on the sidewalks of a major city from time to time and suck in some of those wonderful emissions of an ICE vehicle" 

And people such as yourself have likely never lived in rural areas where coal fired power stations are located, where as everyone no matter where they live is acquainted with City pollution having either grown up there or having to visit the place from time to time.

Every new EV sold that is charged overnight on off-peak rates is adding to the emissions in areas where such power stations are located. Ever since they connected businesses and houses to the grid over 100 years ago power companies worldwide have faced one major problem: The majority of people sleep during the night and electricity consumption goes way way down and potential profit is lost. EV's have finally addressed an issue that has always plagued the industry. Ask anyone old enough that grew up in cities that suffered the choking pollution during winter when people used coal and briquettes for heating. What you have now is nothing in comparison. That is history that is being recreated outside of your precious city where the real exhaust pipes of your EV's are. Hangs around like a fog particularly at night and on cold mornings and when it's really bad you can smell sulfur which is part of the composition. More acid rain yay!
Without exception It's always about money first.

author: @jameshughes3014
text:
I owned one. Many of the things you're saying don't match my experience, or the experience of my friends who owned one.
 I would not say repair is more expensive, because it's so seldom needed that over the life of the vehicle I saved so much money. Repair and maintenance on a gas car honestly is more expensive.
My insurance was cheaper for that car than for a comparable gas model.
My car was 3 years old when I bought it , I kept it for years.
None of that means that they are perfect, there's surely things that need to improve. 
But it was a lot cheaper to own, more reliable, and less of a hassle. Plus it was zippy, and the heat comes on instantly. It was really nice.
I am from the US though, electricity is cheaper here, so that was probably part of the savings.

author: @jb-dn3ct
text:
Brilliant navigation of a volatile subject, i remember learning about the cholera outbreak in londen years ago working on the water mains, quite interesting.

Failure is not a crime, a wise man once said to me if someone makes a big mistake at a work place that results in say damage or someone getting hurt then should they be sacked or not? Logically they're less likely to make the same mistakes again as bringing someone new in as failure is the best teacher, if they don't learn from their mistakes then it's time to go.

As for the whole ev or ice argument i think you hit the nail on the head, managing the waste is going to have far more of an impact than trying to rush in new technologies and try to figure it out as we go along, we have 2 cars both 20 years old, my volvo im planning to keep as long as life will allow, I've already rebuilt the engine among other substantial repairs, the other car is a honda which I've needed to weld the sills up recently due to some serious corrosion but other than my time and effort the maintenance costs me a fraction of what most need to pay using a garage, there's nothing on an old ice car i can't fix/rebuild but i wouldn't have a clue with an ev, neither could i afford to have one maintained at a garage, I'm all for those who feel the need to own one, but it upsets me when the current trend looks like it's moving towards eventually forcing the market so that people can only buy ev, the problems are only small currently but the cracks are starting to appear and it will get worse before it gets better

I think you are missing the main point of an electric car, there is about a 10 fold reduction in moving and wearing parts.

There is not much to do, the brakes last a lot longer as regenerative braking reduces the use, there is little oil in the engine, just brake fluid, filters and coolant to change.

author: @toyotaboyhatman
text: This feels skewed. I think the repair cost is getting skewed by the small percentage of cars needing a complete battery replacement because of manufacturing issues. Ev's have very few moving parts. Most of them don't even need brake pads replaced for 100k miles because of regenerative braking. And the 3.8 years you're seeing is likely smaller battery cars like the base model Nissan leaf with smaller capacity so of course the battery is going to wear out quicker if it's been discharged more often. Anything current with a 250 mile range should be able to easily go 200k miles before the battery needs replacing. An ICE car would likely need a major engine overhaul or replacement at that mileage too. The difference with EV, by the time you need a new battery, the lithium cell cost will have dropped in cost (as it has historically done since its inception because of more demand).

replies.author: @mark_5588
replies.text: Your 25 year old car was once the enemy of people who understood carburetors and didn't want to learn how that new fuel injection with sensors everywhere worked.  I can't tell you how often I heard "I can fix it with a screwdriver while you need that fancy, expensive laptop".  The other was "my carb can make just as much power and get the same mileage".  No, they couldn't and no one today would listen to that and think that person was anything other than deeply mistaken.  25 years from now, no one will do anything but laugh when retelling stories of people who think EVs are no more efficient, or are dirtier than ICE vehicles.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I absolutely agree with that

replies.author: @duicenasty1078
replies.text: @mark_5588 100% Agree with that people get stuck with what they know works. I used to work converting cars to run on LPG. Converting cars from the 60s up to fresh out the factory with 20 miles on the clock in 2010. From 1960s Rolls Royce to Austin mini,  V12 sports cars to 40Ft Winnebago.  Also used to fit LPG to diesel artic lorries and coaches to clean up the emission and get 9-15% more fuel efficiency. So I have worked on many types of vehicles of all ages. Condemned a fare few to the scrap yard that mechanically were good but due to something like a sensor that's basically a hall effect sensor costing more to replace than the car is worth.  Now with programed obsolescence coded into ECU's. Error codes pop up on a set time for parts to be replaced when they are working fine.   Progress for people not for profit.  That's why i like my old car, i know how to fix it with a screw driver.

replies.author: @damianmoore6237
replies.text: @orionbetelgeuse1937 I'd say efficiency (energy and cost) is also a big factor for drivers. Combustion engines are still stuck at 20-30% power efficiency whereas EVs start at 90+%. Then if you take into account delivery of that energy you're losing about another 50% on top with fossil fuels. There are obviously losses in the electrical grid too but it's a different level and getting lower carbon all the time.

author: @secretrat
text:
You could make this same argument about any "disruptive" technology. Geoffrey A. Moore discusses this process of innovation in his book, "Crossing the Chasm." When an innovative technology is first released, there are early adopters who are so eager to embrace it that they willingly suffer the pain that goes with it. Without those early adopters, there would be no bridgehead from where the majority of eventual customers will accept it themselves. Crossing this chasm is a necessary part of all innovation before it reaches the state of broad market acceptance.

Maybe the current state of the EV industry is an unremembered lesson from history, or perhaps it is just the nature of human resistance to change. I do see a difference from when I read Moore's book 20 or so years ago, and that isn't the width of the chasm but rather the shorter time it now takes to bridge it.

I expect my next vehicle will be an EV. I don't own one now, mostly because I haven't yet realized my expected return on investment of my ICE vehicle. At some point that balance will shift and dispite the lack of readiness of infrastrure, I'll be buying an EV. And I expect that I will suffer some pain of being a (not quite so) early adopter.

But yes still many places lack even that.

author: @normcfu
text: 7:58 One of the reasons the average age of an EV is 3-4 years is because most EVs have been build and sold in the last 3 years as the growth continues to be exponential.  These cars are still on the road, not scrapped.  There aren't many old EVs because there were't built.  Normally I find your analysis good, but when it comes to EVs you need to sit back and think more.

replies.author: @DisorderedArray
replies.text: Yeah, he totally leaned into the claim that EVs are scrapped immediately after reaching their average on-road age. It's not even mathematically possible.

replies.author: @pmac6584
replies.text: Exactly correct. I was about to make this comment and saw yours. This is an example of a daft conclusion from correct data.

replies.author: @audiowan
replies.text: I see 10 year old Nissan leaf's still driving very faithfully and (due to regenerative braking) at 120k still no need of brake jobs, no muffler repairs, no oil changes, save lots of money for the owner,  and even charged via. solar panels on roof of flat.  Still after 10 years car gets 30 miles per charge.  We must switch away from petroleum burning vehicles.  Visualize a 1 Km round pipe sucking clean air in and expelling  toxic gas 24/7 (this is what is happening right as we speak.)  World wide ,  at any given time .  Cheers

author: @10sheds21
text: I usually watch your videos while eating my tea, fairly stomach churning today. However a good many years ago i lived without drainage. The key to successful recycling was to separate the urine from the solid hence the problem we created with the flushing toilet. Looked as a resource it was excellent urine straight on the compost heap and solids composted separately always well covered after 18 months wonderful. Batteries might be worn out for cars but  they could be used in other ways

author: @Jimbo878
text: I observed years ago that I'd be travelling up the motorway (12 miles) to get to work, in my 5 seater car alone, the motorway lanes going in the opposite direction was busy too, people alone in their 5 seater car travelling into the area around where I lived - going to work 🤔 the period when most vehicles are on the road is people going to, and then returning from work, passing each other in opposite directions!. Some countries are set up with manufacturing/industry/services in large clusters with towns and cities nearby, people can bicycle, scooter, or travel on trains to and from work... Shenzhen is one example.

author: @steverichmond7142
text: You are absolutely wrong on everything.  I've had EV's for 11 years. They never break down. My insurance is cheaper. My Tesla has done 110k. and my MG has done 20k. You are wrong about the average life of an ev. Please look at Fully Charged for information about battery recycling. Your information is dangerous as it looks like you're working for Big Oil.

replies.author: @ksgraham3477
replies.text:
You do not drive much when one extrapolated the years and miles.
EVs are OK for stay at Homer's that just run to market and back. Not for people that need range, or live in the country.

replies.author: @steverichmond7142
replies.text: I have EV vans in my construction business that regularly travel 500 miles per week. My Tesla Model X travels 300 miles on one journey every month. I used to have a Mercedes S type diesel that did 50k+ a year but I thought that was irresponsible, so I got 2 EVs and cut down on my mileage. I live in rural Scotland where there are a lot of EV's. Please see Fully Charged.@ksgraham3477

author: @chrisstevens2

author: @h-j.k.8971
text:
hm. So my electric Car is 9 years old and is still going strong. I have invested a couple of K´s in the last few years which brought it up to date. I drive it every day and now again on long trips. The upkeep works out to be about half of what an ICE car would be. Besides charging at home there is a large network of charging stations out there that I use when on long journeys.
 But then, I don´t live in the UK.

replies.author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
replies.text: It is not so much of a problem in UK , as far as I know (I mean , people complain about cost of maintenance and repairs and problems with charging infrastructure , but it can work , besides occasional electric car fire)  , however , if you are in the US , try to use that car and drive to Mexico , or further south , or , if in EU , try to drive that to , let's say Kazakhstan... I'm not against it , as far as circumstances allow it (like stable grid and charging infrastructure , living standard , etc..) and I like silent drive and great torque , but I don't like the cost of it (I never use financing/loans) and I don't like limits of it. If batteries were out of picture and replaced with something more sustainable and more dependable (like hydrogen) - I'm all for it (providing that price of that is comparable to ICE cars today).

replies.author: @h-j.k.8971
replies.text: @pedjamilosavljevic6235 Another hydrogen dreamer.

replies.author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
replies.text: @h-j.k.8971 I used it as an example of existing technology. Not as axiom , at least in current state of affairs , regarding hydrogen (production , manipulation and transport , storage , etc...). Batteries evolve , I get it and when they invent and make some that can last 25+ years in any condition,  charged within 10 minutes , with dependable and sustainable infrastructure - I'm all for it. Preferably from carbon , because we have it more than we can handle and some other abundant material (safe-ish to use , of course). With that comes comparable price of a vehicle to today's ICE vehicles. Electric motor (or motors) are better than ICE in every respect. Batteries , compared to standard fuels are not .

replies.author: @keXen
replies.text: He is reading the stats wrong. The sales of EVs in the US is goving rapidly. If the majority of EVs are sold the last two years, then the average age will be very low. There are not a lot of 20 year old EVs that are pushing the average age up yet..

replies.author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
replies.text: @keXen There will always be very few Ev's with 20 years of age , as well as most of newly produced ICE cars , because the car industry is going in direction of under 10 years ownership , or partial ownership of the car (in terms of programmed failure of crucial components). After 20 years , current battery technology , even with extremely low mileage cars , battery pack will be done anyway you cut it. Repčaement would be expensive , if possible at all (lack of spares and/or qualified people to do it).
